topic: Developments in Christian Thought: Augustine, Death & Afterlife, Knowledge of God
subject: religious-studies

items.q: What is the problem of evil?
items.correct: If God is all-loving, all-knowing and all-powerful, why does evil and suffering exist? Logically, all four cannot be true together without explanation.
items.misconception: It is the question of whether evil people will go to hell.
items.random_wrong: It is the question of whether the devil really exists.
items.why: Students treat it as a moral or eschatological question. It is actually a logical challenge to classical theism — and the central problem theodicies try to solve.

items.q: What is the doctrine of the Trinity?
items.correct: God is one being existing in three persons (Father, Son, Holy Spirit) — distinct in person but united in essence; Christians remain monotheistic.
items.misconception: Christians believe in three different gods.
items.random_wrong: The Trinity refers to heaven, earth and hell.
items.why: Students treat 'three persons' as 'three gods'. The orthodox position is one God in three distinct persons sharing one substance — a notoriously difficult concept students often misrepresent as polytheism.

items.q: What was the Augustine vs Pelagius dispute about original sin?
items.correct: Augustine argued humans inherit a corrupted nature from Adam (original sin) and cannot achieve salvation without grace; Pelagius argued humans retain free will to choose good without divine intervention and that sin is imitated, not inherited.
items.misconception: Original sin means Christians believe babies are evil.
items.random_wrong: Pelagius and Augustine agreed humans are born innocent.
items.why: Original sin is about inherited nature/condition, not personal guilt of infants. The Pelagian controversy shaped Western Christianity — Augustine won, but the question of how far human nature is corrupted remains theologically contested.

items.q: Is purgatory a second chance for the damned to be saved?
items.correct: No — in Catholic teaching purgatory is the purification of those who die in God's grace but imperfectly purified; it is preparation for heaven, not an appeal court for the damned.
items.misconception: Purgatory is where the damned get a second chance to repent and escape hell.
items.random_wrong: Purgatory is the Protestant name for heaven.
items.why: Purgatory presupposes salvation: only the saved pass through it. Students recast it as post-mortem probation, which both Catholic doctrine and its Protestant critics would reject as a description.

items.q: In Calvin's theology, can believers earn their election through good works?
items.correct: No — election is God's sovereign, unconditional choice made before creation; good works are the fruit and evidence of election, never its cause.
items.misconception: Calvin taught that doing enough good works persuades God to elect you to salvation.
items.random_wrong: Calvin taught that everyone is automatically elected to salvation.
items.why: Unconditional election is the heart of Calvin's predestinarian logic — grace precedes and produces works. Reversing the order turns Calvin into the very works-righteousness he attacked.
The answer examiners want is: Augustine. “Aquinas” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: When did Luther post the 95 Theses?
items.why: Correct answer: 1517. “1492” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many theses did Luther post?
items.why: The real figure is 95 theses. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
